chunk_summary: Macrocytosis workup emphasizes targeted evaluation for folate and B12 deficiencies, avoiding folate-only treatment to prevent neurological damage, especially in pediatric cases with specific clinical features.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical significance of macrocytosis and its common causes in pediatric patients.
Differentiate between folate and B12 deficiencies using first-line and advanced diagnostic tests.
Recognize the risks of folate-only treatment and the importance of addressing B12 deficiency first.
Apply appropriate treatment strategies for pediatric patients with macrocytosis and related deficiencies.
key_facts: Macrocytosis is commonly caused by folate deficiency, B12 deficiency, mixed deficiencies, or marrow disorders in pediatric patients.
Clinical features of B12 deficiency include glossitis, hyperpigmented knuckles, hypotonia, developmental regression, paresthesias, and seizures.
First-line tests include serum B12 and folate levels; homocysteine and methylmalonic acid can help differentiate deficiencies when results are unclear.
Folate-only treatment can correct anemia but worsen neurological damage in unrecognized B12 deficiency.
In cases of combined deficiencies, B12 should be replaced first or both should be replaced together.
A 9-month-old exclusively breastfed by a vegetarian mother with macrocytosis and developmental regression requires prompt B12 replacement, often parenteral if neurologic symptoms are present.
reference_quote: A high-stakes pitfall is giving folate alone without excluding B12 deficiency. Folate can correct anemia while allowing neurologic injury from unrecognized B12 deficiency to progress.
